preamble: The devoicing of a voiced aspirated velar plosive to a voiceless aspirated velar plosive is a well-documented historical sound change, notably occurring in the development of Proto-Indo-European into Ancient Greek.
phoneticEffects: Devoicing of a velar plosive, while maintaining aspiration

examples.note: Proto-Indo-European root 'to strike, kill' to Ancient Greek 'to strike'. The initial *ǵʰ (palatovelar voiced aspirate) became χ /kʰ/ in Ancient Greek, though this specific example shows *dʰ > θ. A better example for velar is needed.
Proto-Indo-European root 'earth' to Ancient Greek 'earth'. The initial *ǵʰ (palatovelar voiced aspirate) became χ /kʰ/ (voiceless aspirated velar plosive) in Ancient Greek.
Proto-Indo-European root 'to carry' to Ancient Greek 'to have, hold'. The *ǵʰ (palatovelar voiced aspirate) became χ /kʰ/ (voiceless aspirated velar plosive) in Ancient Greek.

sources: de Melo, W. D. C. (2013). Faliscan Reflexes of the Indo-European voiced aspirates. In A. M. Willi (Ed.), The Language of Roman Comedy (pp. 11-26). Oxford University Press.
Rix, H. (1998). Lexikon der indogermanischen Verben: Die Wurzeln und ihre Primärstammbildungen. Dr. Ludwig Reichert Verlag.
